Apps

An app connects AgentOS to an external platform. Read this once, build an app.

An app is a directory with a readme.md (identity + config) and one or more *.py files (tools).

The engine reads both at boot:

  • readme.md frontmatter → identity, connections, test config
  • *.py files → tools (via AST parsing, never executed at boot)

How tool discovery works

Section titled “How tool discovery works”

There is no manifest listing tools. The engine walks every *.py file in the app directory, parses it as AST (without importing it), and treats any top-level function carrying an SDK decorator (@returns, @provides, @connection, @timeout) as a tool. The tool name is the Python function name — the filename is irrelevant to routing. A request to apps.run({app:"my-app", tool:"list_items"}) resolves to whichever file defines def list_items(...) with a decorator.

Because AST discovery happens before import, decorators are no-ops at runtime — they’re markers the engine greps for, not logic that runs. Undecorated functions (including underscore-prefixed helpers) are invisible to the engine regardless of their name.

How apps are found on disk

Section titled “How apps are found on disk”

The engine finds apps by walking the apps/ subdirectory of every source registered on the graph (tag: source, val: address → filesystem path). No sources registered → no apps discovered. The dev.sh tooling registers the local dev source automatically; in production, a source is added via agentos sources add <path>.

Nothing about the shapes an app returns has to exist on the graph first. Shapes land lazily: the first time an app returns @returns("widget"), the Python worker stamps the dict with __shape_yaml__ (the raw inner YAML body read from SHAPE_YAMLS in the generated SDK), and the Rust upsert path byte-compares that body against the shape-def node — identical is a no-op, different overwrites. Running a brand-new app on a wiped database works end-to-end as long as a source is registered; the graph fills itself as data flows.

Two independent things, often confused:

  • Source registration — “where do apps live on disk?” Graph state. Needed before an app can be dispatched.
  • Shape registration — “what does a widget look like?” Lazy, happens on first return. Never needs pre-loading.

Multi-file apps

Section titled “Multi-file apps”

An app with several logical surfaces can split them across files. Claude has an API connector, a CLI connector, and a web connector — all under app id claude:

All three files contribute to a single flat tool namespace keyed on function name. list_models in claude_api.py and list_sessions in claude_code.py are both reachable as tool: "list_models" / tool: "list_sessions" on app claude. Tool names must be globally unique within the app — duplicates are a bug, caught by agent-sdk validate.

Helper functions (any name starting with _) and imports are not tools, so you can share _flatten_content or _parse_row across files without worrying about collisions.

App anatomy

Section titled “App anatomy”

readme.md — identity and docs

Section titled “readme.md — identity and docs”

Required frontmatter fields: id, name.

Connections no longer live in frontmatter — they’re declared at module level in the .py file. See connections.md for the full reference. Tests declared with @test decorators in the .py file too.

Connection auth types:

  • api_key — API key in a header, query param, or body field
  • cookies — browser session cookies (domain required)
  • oauth — OAuth2 refresh token flow

Python module — connection + tools

Section titled “Python module — connection + tools”

Rules:

  • A function becomes a tool when it carries at least one SDK decorator (@returns, @provides, @connection, @timeout). Undecorated functions and _underscore_prefixed helpers are invisible to the engine.
  • Every tool MUST have @returns("shape") or @returns("shape[]") — the decorator the engine reads to know what the tool produces.
  • Every tool MUST accept **params — the engine injects auth, context, and session state there.
  • First line of docstring = tool description (shown in MCP tool schema).
  • Args: section (Google style) = parameter descriptions.
  • Type hints on params = schema types (str, int, bool, float).
  • Default values = optional params; no default = required.
  • The tool name is the function name. Keep function names globally unique within the app, even across multiple .py files.

Decorators

Section titled “Decorators”

@returns(shape) — required on every tool

Section titled “@returns(shape) — required on every tool”

The engine reads this via AST at boot — it’s a no-op at runtime, and its mere presence is what marks a function as a tool.

@provides(service) — provide a standard service

Section titled “@provides(service) — provide a standard service”

Standard service names (import from agentos):

  • web_search, web_read — discovery & retrieval
  • email_lookup — people
  • flight_search, geocoding, map_tiles — travel
  • file_list, file_read, file_info — files
  • cookie_auth, oauth_auth — auth provision (browser cookie stores, OAuth token issuers)
  • login_credentials, password, api_key — credential provision (password managers, keychains). Building a login flow? See How to add login to an app. For reference on credential providers, see Auth Flows § credential providers.
  • llm — LLM inference (see llm.md)
  • cdp_access — access to a live browser via Chrome DevTools Protocol (navigate, evaluate JS, hook fetch, read storage). Provided by Chromium-family browser apps; consumed by RE / scraping / any app that needs in-page work. Name follows the *_auth / *_access convention — the service is “access to a browser”; CDP is the protocol. Contract in the engine repo’s roadmap (core/_roadmap/p2/browser-stack/) until it lands on this page.

The service pattern, in one paragraph

Section titled “The service pattern, in one paragraph”

Apps never name each other. A consumer declares what it needs (services: [llm, cdp_access] in its readme frontmatter, or via an SDK call like await cdp.connect(...)); the engine walks every installed @provides(<name>) app, scores the matches — the user’s default app for that service first (the defaults_to edge) — and dispatches to the winner. The consumer doesn’t import the provider; the provider doesn’t know who’s calling. The engine is the only broker. This is how login_credentials matchmaking works for password managers, how llm matchmaking works for inference — and it’s the same mechanism for every new service you introduce. If you find yourself writing from apps.other_app import foo, stop: the service pattern is almost always what you want instead.

System apps — engine-native providers

Section titled “System apps — engine-native providers”

A smaller set of @provides(X) implementations live in the engine itself, under crates/system-apps/, as Rust closures. These are system apps: compiled in, universally available, not installable / uninstallable / forkable by app authors. They participate in matchmaking exactly like the Python apps you write — services.list_providers sees them, the brokered services.* verbs dispatch to them, the same @provides(X) walk finds them. The difference is in-process: the tool body runs in Rust, not Python.

You don’t build system apps the way you build installed apps; they’re implemented in the engine repo. As an app author, you just see them as providers. The first one is vault (@provides(login_credentials) over the local encrypted credential store). See _roadmap/p2/system-capes/ in the engine repo for the candidate roadmap (url, hash, time).

Consuming a service

Section titled “Consuming a service”

The SDK module (agentos.llm, agentos.cdp, etc.) is a thin dispatch helper — it asks the engine “give me a provider for X” and returns whatever that provider hands back. Each service gets its own module with a typed surface so consumers get LSP-hover docs without knowing which provider answered.

@connection(name) — bind to auth connection

Section titled “@connection(name) — bind to auth connection”

Tells the engine which connection’s credentials to inject into params["auth"].

@timeout(seconds) — override default 30s timeout

Section titled “@timeout(seconds) — override default 30s timeout”

SDK modules

Section titled “SDK modules”

All I/O goes through SDK modules. Never import urllib, requests, subprocess, sqlite3, or os.popen — the engine sandbox blocks them.

http — HTTP requests

Section titled “http — HTTP requests”

URL helpers

Section titled “URL helpers”

The SDK has four URL helpers on http. Use them — never hand-roll URL encoding with urllib (it’s banned in the sandbox).

When you need a dynamic path segment (e.g. name:{company} in a CDN URL), use url.encode(name). When you need a query string, use params= or build_url. There’s almost never a reason to build a URL with f-strings plus encode — the higher-level helpers are cleaner.

Content-Type override — AWS JSON variants, etc.

Section titled “Content-Type override — AWS JSON variants, etc.”

Per-request headers= always beats the Content-Type implied by the body. For AWS endpoints that want application/x-amz-json-1.1 (e.g. Cognito):

You do NOT need a raw-bytes body mode for this — json=dict + headers={"Content-Type": "..."} is enough.

Browser-like headers — set by the connection’s client= kind

Section titled “Browser-like headers — set by the connection’s client= kind”

Cookie-auth sites — especially ones behind Cloudflare, CloudFront, or Vercel — expect browser-grade headers (UA, Sec-CH-UA*, Sec-Fetch-*, Accept-*). Apps don’t compose these per-request any more; the connection’s client= value picks the bundle, and it rides every client.get/post/… call automatically.

client=Header bundleWhen to use
"browser"UA + Sec-CH-UA* + full Sec-Fetch-* navigate bundle + Upgrade-Insecure-Requests + Accept: text/html,...Cookie-authed dashboards, login flows, top-level page scraping
"fetch"UA + Sec-CH-UA* + XHR-style Sec-Fetch-Mode: cors + Accept: application/jsonAJAX-style API calls a real browser’s JS would make
"api" (default)Nothing — caller passes any needed headersREST APIs with key or token auth

Per-call overrides (rare, ~5% of tools):

The engine’s TLS fingerprint (Chrome via wreq) and the SDK’s Python UA/client-hint strings ship as a matched pair. When bumping Chrome, update platform/sdk/python/agentos/client.py::_CHROME_VERSION and crates/exec-executors/src/http.rs::CHROME_EMULATION together.

Multi-request flows — the ambient Jar handles them

Section titled “Multi-request flows — the ambient Jar handles them”

There’s no session context manager. Every client.get/post/... call inside one @connection(...) tool shares the same Jar; cookies rotate naturally across calls:

The engine tracks Set-Cookie responses through the Jar. At tool exit, a snapshot-diff emits {added, changed, removed} as __cookie_delta__, and the engine persists the changes to the credential-store row keyed on (domain, identifier).

client.cookie(name) — look up a single cookie by name

Section titled “client.cookie(name) — look up a single cookie by name”

Returns None outside a tool invocation or on an api-kind connection (no jar). For resolving cookies from scratch, the engine does that automatically when the connection declares auth={"type": "cookies", ...} — apps don’t call a resolver.

Cookie helpers

Section titled “Cookie helpers”

sql — database queries

Section titled “sql — database queries”

Use this instead of import sqlite3. The engine handles path resolution, logging, and permissions.

shell — binary execution

Section titled “shell — binary execution”

Shell interpreters (bash, sh, zsh) are always blocked. Run binaries directly. Use this instead of import subprocess.

crypto — browser cookie decryption

Section titled “crypto — browser cookie decryption”

Used by browser cookie providers. You probably won’t need this directly.

oauth — token exchange

Section titled “oauth — token exchange”

molt — cleaning scraped data

Section titled “molt — cleaning scraped data”

molt() is the universal cleaner — use it for any scraped value. It handles HTML entities, whitespace normalization, and sentinel detection (“N/A”, “not available” → None).

llm — agent workflows

Section titled “llm — agent workflows”

LLM inference (one-shot, multi-turn agent loops with tool calling, structured output). See llm.md for the full reference.

checkpoint — resume multi-phase workflows

Section titled “checkpoint — resume multi-phase workflows”

See llm.md for the full reference — checkpoint lives alongside agent workflows.

Naming rules

Section titled “Naming rules”
WhatConventionExample
Python functions (tools)snake_case, no prefixdef list_posts(**params)
Shape field keys (entity data)camelCase"startDate", "postedBy"
Non-shape keys (API passthrough)Keep source format"access_token" from an API response

Python is snake_case. Data going into the graph is camelCase. That’s it.

No run_ / op_ / do_ prefixes on tool functions. The engine parses Python as text and the tool name is the function name verbatim — async def search_books is called as tool: "search_books". The app id is already the namespace.

Shape conventions

Section titled “Shape conventions”

Shapes define the structure of entities. See the Ontology overview for the full reference (design principles, the also inheritance chain, polymorphic children via shape:, identity rules).

Standard fields (available on all shapes):

  • id (string) — unique identifier
  • name (string) — display name
  • url (string) — canonical URL
  • image (string) — image URL
  • published (datetime) — when created/published
  • content (string) — main text content

Example — returning an event shape:

Common shapes: event, product, person, account, book, email, post, result, webpage, order, review, article, place, domain, channel, conversation, message, file, folder.

Use agent-sdk shapes to list all shapes. Use agent-sdk shapes event to see a shape’s fields and relations.

Annotated examples

Section titled “Annotated examples”

Public API (no auth)

Section titled “Public API (no auth)”

Pattern: No auth, no browser bundle, no @connection. Just client.get().

Cookie-auth (Goodreads)

Section titled “Cookie-auth (Goodreads)”

Pattern: @connection("web") → engine resolves cookies + seeds the ambient Jar; client="browser" supplies the CloudFront-passing header bundle automatically; client.get reads the Jar under the hood. lxml + cssselect for HTML parsing. molt() to clean scraped text.

API-key (Exa)

Section titled “API-key (Exa)”

Pattern: @connection("api") → engine injects API key into params["auth"]["key"]. Header set directly on request.

Common mistakes

Section titled “Common mistakes”

Blocked imports — the sandbox blocks direct network/system access:

snake_case shape fields — shape fields must be camelCase:

Non-shape keys (API passthrough, internal metadata, engine contracts) keep their source format. The validator is shape-scoped — it only flags a snake_case key if the function’s declared @returns shape has a matching camelCase field:

Missing **params:

Missing @returns:

Wrong client= kind on cookie-auth:

Missing await on async SDK calls — the SDK is fully async. A missing await silently returns a coroutine instead of the real response and the failure surfaces downstream as a 'coroutine' object has no attribute 'get' or similar:

The function containing the await must itself be async def. If you’re adding an await to a helper, propagate the cascade all the way up to the decorated tool function. agent-sdk validate catches missing awaits statically.

time.sleep() in an async def — blocks the event loop. Use await asyncio.sleep(...):

http.request("POST", ...) — doesn’t exist. Dispatch by verb:

BeautifulSoup — use lxml with cssselect:

Hardcoded User-Agent — let the connection’s client= kind supply the UA bundle:

run_, op_, do_ prefixes on tool functions — the engine parses Python as text and the tool name is the function name, verbatim. No prefix stripping. The app id is already the namespace — don’t repeat it.

This matters for the readme test: block too — the YAML key must match the Python function name exactly.

Async chain half-converted — if a helper eventually calls client.get/post/…, it must be async def and every caller of it must await it. Adding await to a sync helper raises SyntaxError; forgetting await on an async helper silently returns a coroutine and breaks at the first .get("ok"). There is no middle ground.

agent-sdk validate flags both halves. If you touch one helper in a chain, walk the chain up and down.

No account on a multi-account app — if more than one credential row exists for an app (e.g. Brave synced two Goodreads cookies), the engine refuses to pick. Every apps.run call must include "account": "<name>". This applies even to tools that don’t actually need auth.

Special return keys

Section titled “Special return keys”

Tools can return special keys alongside or instead of shape data:

Testing

Section titled “Testing”

AgentOS tests apps against real platforms, never mocks. The bar is the same one a human gets the first time they try a tool: “did it come back without an error?” If it did, the tool works. If it didn’t, the app is broken and the quality sweep notices.

Two places declare tests, one runner executes them.

1. Declare tests in the readme test: block

Section titled “1. Declare tests in the readme test: block”

Every tool in an app should have a line in the readme frontmatter’s test: block. The key is the tool name; the value is either skip: true, {} (no params), or { params: { … } } with real live inputs.

Real examples — same shape, different domains:

  • apps/media/hackernews/readme.md — public API, no auth.
  • apps/macos/macos-control/readme.md — local shell/OS app.
  • apps/media/goodreads/readme.md — cookie-auth + AppSync GraphQL.

2. agent-sdk validate — static checks

Section titled “2. agent-sdk validate — static checks”

15 static checks, including: banned imports (sandbox), missing await, sync time.sleep in async bodies, tool-name collisions, shape conformance of return dicts, camelCase enforcement, frontmatter field validation. Runs automatically on pre-commit — you will not commit an app that fails these.

3. _quality/bin/record.py — effectiveness sweep

Section titled “3. _quality/bin/record.py — effectiveness sweep”

The quality recorder. On every commit to core/ or apps/ it appends one row to _quality/reports/history.jsonl: it walks every app readme, calls every tool declared in test: through the live engine, records pass/fail, and writes an effectiveness percentage.

Pass = the call returned without a top-level _error. No assertions on output shape. Green means the tool hit the real platform and came back with something.

Tracked over time by the quality regime (_quality/readme.md) as one of three numbers (effectiveness, size, active-surface). Deletion should not drop effectiveness. Additions should not drop it either.

4. agentos call apps — while iterating

Section titled “4. agentos call apps — while iterating”

For single-tool debugging during development:

agentos call apps '{"op":"run",...}' exercises the whole live stack (engine, MCP, Python worker, SDK, auth resolution) and is the closest thing to what a client MCP would do. Use it when the quality sweep flags a tool and you need to see the actual error.

What NOT to do

Section titled “What NOT to do”
  • No unit tests, no mocks. Every test hits the real platform. This is a deliberate project rule. A mocked test that passes while production breaks is worse than no test at all.
  • No assertions on output structure beyond “did not throw”. Shape conformance is already enforced by agent-sdk validate at the AST level; runtime assertions duplicate that and rot faster than the API does.
  • No test-only params. The readme test: block uses the same params a real caller would — public IDs, real queries. If a tool needs auth to be useful, the quality sweep will tell you via the credential-missing error; add the account and rerun.

Quick reference

Section titled “Quick reference”
ImportWhat
from agentos import httpHTTP client (get, post, put, patch, delete, head, client, headers, cookies)
from agentos import sqlDatabase queries (query, execute)
from agentos import shellBinary execution (run)
from agentos import cryptoCrypto operations (pbkdf2, aes)
from agentos import oauthToken exchange (exchange)
from agentos import moltUniversal text cleaner
from agentos import returns, provides, connection, timeoutDecorators
from agentos import web_search, web_read, ...Service names
from agentos import get_cookies, require_cookies, parse_cookieCookie helpers
from agentos import app_error, app_result, app_secretResult helpers
from agentos import parse_int, parse_float, parse_dateType parsers
from agentos import iso_from_ms, iso_from_secondsTimestamp converters
from agentos import clean_text, clean_html, strip_tagsText cleaners
from agentos import llmLLM inference — see llm.md
from agentos import checkpointCheckpoint/resume — see llm.md

CLI commands

Section titled “CLI commands”
Previous
HTTP Next
Agent empathy
